content.text: A CoE that meets ad-hoc will spend most of its meetings reacting to crises. A CoE with a structured operating cadence spends most of its meetings making planned decisions, and crises get handled by a process that already exists rather than being invented each time. The cadence is the CoE's operational heartbeat — it determines what decisions get made at what frequency and who must be in the room.

attrs.alt: CoE Operating Cadence: four nested rhythms — weekly (intake triage, office hours, async standup), bi-weekly (guild sync, tech review board, risk gate panel), monthly (metrics review, demo day, standards update), quarterly (exec steering report, risk register review, training cohort, catalog review)

content.text: The CoE's rhythm is organized in four nested rings. Each ring feeds information upward into the next:
Operational continuity: triage, unblock, check SLAs
Triaged intake queue; resolved blockers; SLA status
Cross-BU coordination: guild sync + technical decisions
RFC decisions; guild updates; Tier 2 gate completions
KPI dashboard; demo recordings; updated standards
Exec report; risk register snapshot; training cohort launch

The nested structure matters: weekly blockers that go unresolved escalate to the bi-weekly guild sync. Monthly metrics that show declining SLA attainment become the lead item in the quarterly exec report. Information flows up; decisions flow down.
The CoE program manager reviews all new advisory requests submitted in the past week and assigns each one:
Classify: Tier 2 gate (risk review required) or Tier 3 advisory (technical guidance only)
Assign owner from the CoE core team
Set committed due date based on SLA (5 business days for Tier 2, 3 business days for Tier 3)
Send acknowledgment to requestor with owner name and due date

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class RequestType(Enum):
    TIER2_GATE  = "tier2_gate"      # 5-day SLA; AI Risk Review Panel
    TIER3_ADVISORY = "tier3_advisory"  # 3-day SLA; CoE architect

@dataclass
class IntakeRecord:
    request_id: str
    title: str
    bu: str
    submitted: date
    request_type: RequestType
    assigned_to: str | None = None
    due_date: date | None = None

    def triage(self, owner: str) -> None:
        self.assigned_to = owner
        sla_days = 5 if self.request_type == RequestType.TIER2_GATE else 3
        # Skip weekends in SLA calculation
        due = self.submitted
        business_days_added = 0
        while business_days_added < sla_days:
            due += timedelta(days=1)
            if due.weekday() < 5:  # Mon–Fri
                business_days_added += 1
        self.due_date = due

def monday_triage(new_requests: list[IntakeRecord], owners: dict[str, str]) -> None:
    """
    owners maps request_type to default owner.
    In practice the PM may override based on workload.
    """
    for req in new_requests:
        owner = owners.get(req.request_type.value, "coe-program-manager")
        req.triage(owner)
        send_acknowledgment(req)   # email/Slack to requestor

def send_acknowledgment(req: IntakeRecord) -> None:
    print(
        f"[{req.request_id}] Assigned to {req.assigned_to}. "
        f"Due: {req.due_date}. "
        f"Track at https://coe-portal.internal/requests/{req.request_id}"
    )

Any engineer in any BU can join the Wednesday office hours. No agenda is required — engineers bring questions, design sketches, or production issues. The CoE architect and at least one guild lead attend. This is the highest-leverage weekly event: it surfaces BU needs the CoE would otherwise discover only after a use case goes wrong.
Three rules for effective office hours:
No decision-making authority at office hours
— questions get answers; approvals go through the intake process. This prevents informal approval paths that bypass governance.
Questions get captured in a shared log
— the same question asked three weeks in a row signals a missing standard document or a platform gap.
Attendance is voluntary but CoE visibility matters
— track attendance by BU quarterly; BUs with zero attendance are the ones that later claim the CoE is inaccessible.
Every CoE core team member posts a Friday update to the CoE Slack channel in a standardized format:

**[Name] — Week of [date]**
✅ Done: [what was completed this week]
🔄 In Progress: [active advisory requests / platform work]
🚧 Blocked: [blockers + who needs to unblock]
📋 Next: [planned for next week]

The async standup is not a status theater exercise — it serves one purpose: blockers surface before the weekend without requiring a synchronous meeting. The program manager reviews all Friday posts and pings relevant parties to resolve blocks before Monday triage.

The guild sync is the primary coordination mechanism between the CoE hub and the BU spokes. All embedded guild leads attend alongside the full CoE core team. Rotating chair (each guild lead chairs once per quarter) prevents the CoE from dominating the agenda.
Build psychological safety; recognize cross-BU collaboration
Surface new use cases, blockers, or requests before they become crises
Preview upcoming changes; gather BU input before publication
New capabilities, deprecations, known issues, upcoming maintenance
Capture follow-ups; assign owners; close loop

The bi-weekly cadence means guild leads get 26 syncs per year. That is enough frequency to maintain alignment without becoming a burden. Guilds that need more frequent coordination should escalate to a dedicated working group, not expand the guild sync.
Tech Review Board (60 min, bi-weekly, alternating with guild sync)

The Tech Review Board (TRB) is the CoE's internal technical decision-making forum. It meets in the alternate weeks from the guild sync so guild leads have time to digest TRB decisions before the next sync. Attendees: Principal AI Architect, MLOps Lead, and invited domain experts.
Platform architecture changes (new services, major version upgrades, infrastructure migrations)
RFC (Request for Comment) documents for new standards or pattern updates
New model evaluations submitted for registry approval
Post-mortems with platform or standards implications
Risk Gate Panel (ad-hoc, within bi-weekly window)

Tier 2 intake requests trigger an ad-hoc Risk Gate Panel convened within the bi-weekly window. Attendees: CoE Lead, AI Ethics Lead, Legal representative, and the BU requestor. The panel reviews the use-case risk memo and outputs one of three verdicts:
Use case may proceed to production
Logged in risk register; re-review date set
May proceed with listed conditions (e.g., PII redaction, human-in-the-loop for high-stakes output, rate limits)
BU acknowledges conditions; compliance checked at deployment gate
Use case may not proceed in current form
BU may resubmit after addressing rejection reasons; CoE architect assigned to advise redesign
The program manager publishes a monthly metrics dashboard 48 hours before the review. The review meeting focuses on exceptions and trends, not on reading numbers aloud:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class CoEMonthlyMetrics:
    month: str                            # "2024-06"

    # Platform SLAs
    gateway_uptime_pct: float             # target >= 99.5
    guardrail_p99_latency_ms: float       # target < 150

    # Advisory throughput
    tier2_completed: int
    tier2_sla_breached: int               # target: 0
    tier3_completed: int
    tier3_sla_breached: int               # target: 0

    # Intake volume
    new_requests: int
    requests_by_type: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=dict)

    # Enablement
    training_completions: int
    office_hours_attendees: int
    community_active_members: int         # posted or reacted in past 30 days

    # BU satisfaction
    nps_score: float | None = None       # optional; from quarterly survey

    def sla_health(self) -> str:
        breached = self.tier2_sla_breached + self.tier3_sla_breached
        if breached == 0 and self.gateway_uptime_pct >= 99.5:
            return "GREEN"
        if breached <= 2 or self.gateway_uptime_pct >= 99.0:
            return "YELLOW"
        return "RED"

When SLA health is RED or the metrics show a sustained trend (e.g., advisory volume growing 20% month-over-month while team size is flat), the monthly review generates a capacity or process proposal that goes to the quarterly exec report.
Demo Day (90 min, open to all engineers)

Demo Day is the CoE's highest-visibility community event. BUs present what they've built — not polished pitches, but honest engineering walkthroughs: what worked, what didn't, and what they'd do differently. Three to five demos, 15 minutes each with open Q&A.
Demo Day serves three functions that no other cadence event does:
— an RAG pattern that the product team invented gets picked up by the data team two days later
— BUs demoing on the LLM gateway and guardrail service make those services concrete for engineers who haven't used them yet
— attendance and demo submission rates are the best leading indicators of whether the CoE is perceived as a partner or a gatekeeper

The CoE program manager reviews all pending document changes (from the GitBook / Confluence backlog) and publishes a changelog to the community channel. Document changes that affect Tier 1 binding rules require a 2-week notice period before taking effect — BUs need time to update their implementations.
The quarterly exec report is the CoE's most important accountability artifact. It must be concise (one slide per section) and answer the questions executives actually ask:
— risk register status: how many approved use cases, how many open findings, how many incidents
— use cases in production vs. 90 days ago; time-to-production trend
— platform cost per BU; advisory SLA attainment; team utilization
— headcount or budget asks tied to specific capacity gaps

# CoE Q3 2024 Steering Report — 1-page summary

## Safety
- Use cases in production: 47 (↑ 12 from Q2)
- Risk register: 3 HIGH, 12 MEDIUM, 32 LOW — 0 CRITICAL
- Open findings: 4 (all MEDIUM; remediation due Q4)
- Incidents: 1 (guardrail latency spike Jul 14; resolved in 4h; post-mortem complete)

## Velocity
- Avg time-to-first-production: 6.2 weeks (↓ from 8.1 weeks in Q2)
- Tier 2 gate SLA attainment: 94% (target: 100%; 2 breaches in July)
- New use case intake: 18 (↑ 5 from Q2)

## Efficiency
- Platform cost per active BU: $2,340/month (↓ 8% from Q2 via model routing optimization)
- Advisory requests: 31 completed, 0 open past SLA as of Sep 30
- CoE team utilization: 87% (guideline: 70–85%)

## Asks
- 1 additional MLOps engineer (advisory queue growing; utilization above guideline)
- Q4 training budget: $45k for 2 new instructor-led cohorts

## Highlights
- Demo Day Sep 26: 6 demos, 140 attendees (highest ever)
- GenAI Foundations cohort: 38 certified (total certified: 112)
Every approved use case at Tier 2 or above gets a quarterly re-review. The AI Ethics Lead and CoE Lead check whether:
The use case is still operating within its approved scope and data classification
The model in use is still on the approved registry (providers deprecate models on short notice)
Open findings from the last approval have been remediated
The estimated monthly volume matches actual usage (cost and risk tier may need updating)
Use cases that have drifted from their approved scope — a BU added a new user population, or started passing a higher data classification — are flagged for re-approval before the next quarter.

The intake process is the mechanism that connects a BU's idea to the CoE's governance and platform. It must be fast enough that BUs don't route around it and rigorous enough that compliance and legal can rely on it.
Used in risk register and exec reports; must be human-readable
For cost attribution and guild lead routing
What the system does; who uses it; what the output action is
Determines guardrail policy and Tier 2 trigger
Tier 2 trigger; routes to data privacy consultation
"Decide" triggers Tier 2 regardless of other fields
Cost cap threshold check; capacity planning
For prioritization; expedited review if < 3 weeks
The requesting BU's embedded guild lead; co-responsible for compliance
Cadences decay. The most common failure modes:
Guild leads give no blockers; same BU leads speak every time
Rotate chair; change format to working-session style; reduce to monthly if BU count < 4
SLA breach rate rising; BUs submitting fewer requests
Add a second triage slot; auto-assign by BU to reduce PM load
< 3 engineers per session for two consecutive months
Survey attendees on timing; consider async Q&A channel as supplement; CoE Lead attends for one quarter to restore trust
Quarterly exec report loses exec attention
Steering Committee reschedules; C-suite delegates to VPs
Compress to one page; lead with safety signal; ask sponsor to re-champion
CoE team members demo their own platform work; give 30-day lead time for BU submissions

The CoE's operating cadence is its organizational metabolism. Weekly triage prevents inbox overflow. Bi-weekly guild syncs maintain the hub-spoke trust relationship. Monthly demos and metrics reviews make the CoE's value visible. Quarterly exec reports maintain political cover and budget continuity.
Run intake triage every Monday — assign owner and SLA due date within 24 hours of submission
Alternate the bi-weekly guild sync and tech review board so guild leads have a week to process platform decisions before the next sync
Track three community health signals monthly: office hours attendance, peer-resolution rate, demo day submissions
Quarterly exec report must answer four questions in one page: Are we safe? Are we moving? Are we efficient? What do we need?
Review cadence health quarterly; kill or reform any recurring meeting that has become status theater
